meta.title: Mercedes S-Class W222 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Mercedes S-Class W222 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Mercedes S-Class W222 — from the 3.0‑litre V6 diesel and plug‑in hybrid to the 6.0‑litre V12 biturbo, plus the 2017 facelift that brought the M256 straight‑six and M176 V8. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the flagship that set new standards for luxury. Backed by 276+ real UK W222 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes S-Class W222 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W222 specifically — from the pre‑facelift M276 V6, M278 V8 and OM642 V6 diesel to the facelift M256 straight‑six, M176 V8 and OM656 straight‑six diesel, plus the M157, M177 and M279 AMG V8s and V12.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All engine families are in Case B — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for any of these families. Ratings are derived from forum and specialist sources. The OM642 V6 diesel is well‑proven but has known oil cooler seal issues. The OM656 straight‑six diesel (2017+) has reported camshaft and rocker wear around 100,000 miles. The M276 V6 and M278 V8 are generally solid; the M157 AMG V8 is robust but has oil vapor separator and rear‑main seal failure risks. All ratings are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

overview.intro: The W222 is the sixth generation of the Mercedes‑Benz S‑Class, produced from 2013 to 2020 as the successor to the W221. Designed during 2009 by Il‑hun Yoon and Robert Lešnik, it set new benchmarks for luxury, technology and refinement. A significant facelift in April 2017 brought new powertrains: the M256 straight‑six petrol with 48V mild‑hybrid technology, the M176 4.0‑litre V8 and the OM656 straight‑six diesel. At the top of the range sat the V12‑powered S 600, Maybach S 600 and S 65 AMG.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W221/W222 • Predecessor: W221 • Successor: W223 • Sibling body styles in this generation: V222 (long wheelbase), X222 (Maybach), C217 (coupé), A217 (cabriolet)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W222 S‑Class generated an estimated 276+ UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED — PLACEHOLDER], concentrated on the S 350 d and S 400 d diesel variants as these flagship saloons enter more affordable second‑hand ownership.

items.quote: The 3.0‑litre straight‑six diesel — 340 bhp, 700 Nm of torque, and the most refined diesel ever fitted to an S‑Class. The OM656 replaced the OM642 V6 in 2017 and delivers seamless straight‑six smoothness, effortless torque and genuine 40+ mpg economy in a 2‑tonne luxury saloon.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless diesel torque with straight‑six refinement and class‑leading economy.

items.quote: The 3.0‑litre straight‑six with 48V mild‑hybrid technology — 367 bhp plus 21 bhp EQ Boost, 500 Nm of torque, and the first straight‑six petrol from Mercedes since the M104. It's smooth, responsive and technologically advanced, with the 48V system making stop‑start virtually undetectable.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want modern straight‑six refinement with mild‑hybrid efficiency.

items.quote: The 2.2‑litre four‑cylinder diesel hybrid — 204 bhp system output. It's the entry‑point to W222 ownership, but the OM651 is an ageing unit with rear‑mounted timing chain issues, and the hybrid system adds further complexity. Low UK sales mean parts and specialist knowledge are thin on the ground.
items.whoItsFor: buyers on a tight budget who understand the OM651's reputation — but the OM642 or OM656 are better bets.

items.quote: The 6.0‑litre V12 biturbo — 530–630 bhp, up to 1,000 Nm of torque. When something fails — and it will be expensive — only V12‑trained specialists should touch it. Parts are rare, labour is intensive, and the S 65's seven‑speed AMG gearbox is a specialist item in itself.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts who understand that V12 ownership comes with six‑figure maintenance budgets.

items.quote: The 3.0‑litre V6 diesel — 258 bhp, 620 Nm of torque, and the most common diesel in the pre‑facelift UK W222 parc. The OM642 is a proven unit with well‑documented fixes, and the 7G‑TRONIC gearbox is robust. Oil cooler seals are the only real weakness — a £800–£1,400 job.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want proven diesel V6 refinement without the facelift OM656's higher complexity or purchase price.

items.quote: The 4.0‑litre V8 — 469 bhp, 700 Nm of torque, with cylinder deactivation that shuts off four cylinders under light load. It's more powerful and efficient than the M278 it replaced, and the classic 'S 560' badge carries real heritage. Often overshadowed by the AMG models, but it's the sweet spot of the V8 range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 power and presence without the AMG running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Watch — ageing four‑cylinder in a flagship
Best Diesel — proven V6, well‑understood fixes
PHEV Watch — battery is the wild card
Solid V8 — robust, but costly to run
Entry AMG — fast, but watch for oil leaks
AMG Value — 612 bhp, AMG running costs
Facelift Diesel — refined, but watch for cam wear

economicsRule.title: The W222 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,500 OM642 recon against a £15,000–£40,000 W222 sits at 8–37% of the car's value — comfortably within the 70% Mercedes threshold. Even the £8,000–£18,000 M279 V12 recon sits at 20–120% against a £15,000 V12 — on a sub‑£20,000 V12, that brushes our 70% threshold. Factor in air suspension, ABC hydraulics and electronics — the S‑Class' non‑engine costs can rival the engine itself.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W222 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S 350 BlueTEC (OM642) — proven V6 diesel, well‑understood fixes, robust 7G‑TRONIC.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W222 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S 65 AMG (M279 V12) — V12 running costs, specialist‑only maintenance, 7‑speed AMG gearbox.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W222 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: S 350 BlueTEC (OM642) — the default UK diesel choice, proven reliability, and the cheapest six‑cylinder to maintain.

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (Pre‑Facelift Diesel)
problems.recommendation: On any OM642 W222 over 80,000 miles, inspect for oil leaks around the V of the engine — £800–£1,400 now is far cheaper than the £3,000–£5,500 replacement that follows if oil loss causes engine damage.

problems.title: OM656 Camshaft & Rocker Wear (Facelift Diesel)
problems.recommendation: On any OM656 W222, listen for popping noises from the intake and address any rough idle promptly — £1,500–£2,500 now is far cheaper than the £3,500–£6,500 replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

problems.title: M157 Oil Vapor Separator & Rear‑Main Seal (Pre‑Facelift AMG)
problems.recommendation: On any M157 S 63 AMG, monitor oil consumption and inspect for oil leaks regularly — £2,000–£4,000 now is far cheaper than the £6,000–£10,000 replacement that follows if oil loss causes bearing damage.

problems.title: W222 Air Suspension & ABC Hydraulics
problems.recommendation: Inspect the air suspension regularly and address any sagging or compressor noise immediately — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 overhaul that follows a complete system failure. This is the biggest non‑engine cost on the W222.

replacementCosts.h2: W222 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline — no Engine Page exists yet for any of these families. These costs are derived from the EM_Proprietary_Data Table A — Engine Replacement Economics for comparable engines, adjusted for the W222's flagship‑class labour access and marked [MB-QUOTE] accordingly. All figures are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W222 four‑door saloon and V222 long‑wheelbase. The C217 coupé and A217 cabriolet share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since the coupé and cabriolet have different body structures and labour access.
